You were dead. Not dying, not struggling, not wandering in confusion—but dead. Paul opens Ephesians 2 with a diagnosis so severe it strips away every illusion of spiritual self-help. Your trespasses and sins had killed you. You were enslaved to the patterns of this world and subject to the one who rules the power of the air, following desires and thoughts that marked you as a child of wrath. There is no softer language here, no room for the thought that you might have been mostly okay, mostly on track. You were, by nature, destined for judgment. And so were all of us. Paul includes himself in this verdict: "All of us also lived among them." This is not about condemning others or standing outside the disaster looking in. This is about recognizing the depth of the condition we all shared before God intervened.

But then comes the word that changes everything: "But because of His great love for us, God, who is rich in mercy." This is not a response to your seeking, your improvement, or your religious effort. God moved toward you while you were dead—completely unable to resurrect yourself. He made you alive together with Christ. He seated you with Christ in the heavenly realms. And He did it all by grace, through faith alone, as a gift you could never earn or deserve. Salvation is not even yours in the sense that you can claim credit for it; it is "not from yourselves." This is the scandal of the gospel. You do not save yourself. You do not even contribute meaningfully to your own salvation. Grace does not meet you halfway. It reaches into your grave and pulls you out.

This grace is not random or impersonal. Paul tells us why God did this: "in order that in the coming ages He might display the surpassing riches of His grace." You exist as a living testament to what God's kindness looks like. And this grace immediately bears fruit in a new way of living. You are "God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance as our way of life." This is crucial: good works are not the price of salvation; they are the evidence and expression of it. You were made for them. God has already prepared them for you.

But there is one more layer to this chapter that demands attention: your reconciliation is not simply vertical—between you and God—but horizontal. If you are a Gentile, remember that you were once separated from Christ entirely, alienated from God's people, without hope. But Christ's blood brought you near. He Himself is your peace. He tore down the wall of hostility that stood between Jew and Gentile by abolishing the law of commandments through His flesh. This is staggering: Christ's death reconciles you not only to God but to the whole family of God. The church is now one new humanity, one body, one household, one building with Christ as the cornerstone. You are no longer strangers. You are citizens of God's kingdom, members of His family, part of the temple where He dwells by His Spirit. This is not metaphor. This is your new identity, purchased by blood, built on the foundation of apostles and prophets, held together and growing into something holy and eternal. You belong.
